[21:20] diet bandwagon, fixing and health coaching their clients.
[21:25] And they're telling all these patients to have 150 grams of protein every day.
[21:32] When patients have significant metabolic dysfunction, that is a huge overdose of protein.
[21:40] And what happens to excess proteins when the body can't use them for protein?
[21:48] Gluconeogenesis happens.
[21:50] That means those proteins are converted to glucose, especially if that person has also
[21:56] cut carbohydrates back in a significant way.
[22:01] So if, if your patient is, um, trying to eat better, trying to lose weight, trying
[22:10] to get their thyroid under control, trying to get balanced hormones, they're working

[22:26] and they start cutting carbs and they start increasing protein because they're supposed
[22:31] somebody do the math, divide 150 by seven.
[22:39] Don't make me do that in my head.
[22:40] Somebody do that.
[22:42] So they're eating 150 grams of protein, trying to force that much every single day.
[22:49] What you getting an easy 21 point something.
[22:52] That's 21 ounces of meat a day.
[22:55] Tell me how any of us are going to eat 20 ounces of meat.
[22:59] That's more than a pound.
[23:03] Like that's a huge quantity of meat.
[23:09] And even if it's not me, you're eating four or five, six eggs.
[23:14] You're eating eight or 10, 12 ounces of steak or hamburger, and then bacon
[23:21] and cheese, like whatever other proteins you're getting.
[23:25] I, I am barely able to get my bare necessity of eight ounces of meat in a day.
[23:33] I can't eat more than that.
[23:36] If I try to eat more than that, I promise you, I will be as big as a barn because
[23:41] all that excess protein will be converted to glucose.
[23:45] Think about, think about it this way.
[23:49] Uh, I often describe this as the path of least resistance.
[23:54] If you mound up your plane on the beach where it's summer, let's go to the beach.
[23:59] So we're sitting on the beach and we pile up this big pile of sand and you
[24:05] take your water bottle and you pour out water and it just runs down the
[24:09] sides of your sand pile.
[24:13] Where does the water go?
[24:16] It follows the path of least resistance.
[24:19] Correct.
[24:21] If it gets to a place where there's a big hump, is the water going to run
[24:26] over the top of that hump?
[24:28] Absolutely not.
[24:29] It's going to go around the path of least resistance.
[24:32] It's going to follow the downward gravity pull that water follows.
[24:39] Our bodies do the same thing.
[24:42] Carbohydrates are the easiest thing to break down into glucose.
[24:45] They're so easy that it begins in the mouth with salivary, amylase, and
[24:51] um, other enzymes that start breaking down carbohydrates.
[24:56] As we swallow, get food into the stomach and the small intestine,
[25:02] more carbohydrates are broken down into glucose and the process then
[25:07] begins to shunt the glucose into the bloodstream.
[25:12] So glucose goes up, that triggers an insulin response.
[25:17] Insulin comes out and says, hey, bud, you don't belong here.
[25:20] Let's go over here into this fat sale and store you because I can't
[25:24] use you right now.
[25:25] And so all carbohydrates go through that process.
[25:29] If we over consume proteins, that same process occurs.
[25:33] It's just a little bit slower.
[25:36] So glucose, carbohydrate to glucose is about a two hour cycle.
[25:40] That's why mainstream nutrition advice says eat about every two-ish hours.
[25:44] So you can fuel that hamster wheel, but proteins will take about four or
[25:54] five hours to go through gluconeogenesis and raise glucose.
[25:59] So type ones know this type one diabetic patients understand fully or most of
[26:07] them do that they will also get spiked levels of glucose when they have lots
[26:16] of protein in their diets.
[26:18] Now for children, it's generally not as significant because what are
[26:25] their proteins going to growth development?
[26:28] They are going to utilize a lot more protein than adults will.
[26:34] So you got to keep that in mind.
[26:36] Many of our type ones that we deal with our kids.
[26:40] And so that is something to keep in the back of your mind.

[26:47] the majority of adults don't need anywhere near that 150 grams of protein
[26:52] a day because it will be converted into glucose glucose.

[32:47] And that came to 57 grams of protein, but I ate half a protein bar.
[32:53] So early in the day, around 11 or 11 thirties, thinking I would take my
[32:58] vitamins, which I got distracted with yard work and health work.
[33:01] And I've never went back and took my vitamins.
[33:03] Um, but I had half a protein bar.
[33:06] And so that gave me a total of about 70 grams of protein for the day.
[33:13] So I was over yesterday.
[33:15] So today I've really paid attention and not had any protein all day today.
[33:20] So I can get back where I should be.
[33:22] So when we think about eating, um, the focus should be proteins, but we also
[33:31] don't have to be targeted on today.
[33:35] Like I will eat less protein today because I had a little bit extra yesterday.
[33:40] And I will try to maintain that balance over the week.
[33:44] So the body doesn't run on a 24 hour clock.
[33:47] It runs on a forever clock.
[33:49] So if you're less today and more tomorrow, okay.
[33:55] Um, and that's what a lot of us as OCD kind of, we got to get it right.
[34:02] Uh, often believe we've got to follow a rule.
[34:05] We got to follow a protocol.
[34:07] It's not like that the body is going to function over many hours, many days
[34:14] with the nutrition it's provided.
[34:16] Now, if I consistently get 50 grams of protein, I'm going to suffer.
[34:22] My, my cells won't be replicating appropriately that phospholipid bilayer.
[34:28] The protein it takes to grow multiple cells will slow down, um, bone
[34:33] health, hormone health, muscle health will all be in jeopardy because
[34:37] I'm not providing adequate nutrition.
[34:40] So it's, it's, we have to understand that we're on a continuum when
[34:45] we're talking about eating and maintaining healthy nutrition.
[34:50] Does that make sense?
[34:55] So make sure patients understand protein is king and then fats are crucial.
[35:00] So if you go to my, uh, Facebook page and you look at my post from
[35:05] yesterday with my food, uh, my, uh, my chronometer graphs are there.
[35:09] You can see the nutrition.

[35:56] What's the first step of protein absorption?
[36:01] Chewing.
[36:02] If we are, if we are eating, I know y'all have done this.
[36:06] I've done it, uh, in my jobs, in my life, I'm in a rush getting, when I had kids
[36:15] at home and I was younger, number one, I didn't eat the way I do now.
[36:19] And so we would pull through drive-throughs and grab a burger and fries
[36:23] and eat on the way to something like we wolf things down in a hurry, trying
[36:29] to get through life and get to the next thing and get to the kid to the
[36:32] next thing and get to this ball game and get to that dance recital.
[36:36] Like life is busy and crazy and hectic.
[36:39] And so eating, wolfing down things like that is going to inhibit good absorption,
[36:47] mostly because you're not chewing well.
[36:49] You're, you're literally and chunking it.
[36:53] Um, and, and it goes down still in large chunks, the larger the pieces we swallow,
[37:01] the harder it is for hydrochloric acid to break that down into something smaller.
[37:07] So, uh, I remember as kids, uh, everybody used to go around.
[37:11] You're supposed to chew your food 32 times or 54 times or whatever it was.
[37:15] I don't even remember the number now, but, uh, used to the kids would always
[37:19] talk about, you're supposed to choose so many times and you got to chew
[37:22] better and dah, dah, dah, well, now there was a reason for that.
[37:27] I don't really care about the number of times, but you need to chew
[37:30] adequately to crush your food.
[37:33] So it can be absorbed.
[37:34] The next step is of course, hydrochloric acid.
[37:37] If we're not consuming adequate salt, sodium chloride, sodium goes mostly
[37:44] toward muscle and other stuff.
[37:46] Chloride mostly becomes hydrochloric acid.
[37:50] So people that are having low salt intake because salt is going to kill us.
[37:55] All right.
[37:56] Um, have a lot of trouble with digestion, people with reflux and
[38:00] constipation and terrible gut health.
[38:03] They struggle adding salt in because they've believed all this time
[38:08] that salt was bad for them.
[38:09] They start adding salt in and many of their symptoms began subsiding when
[38:14] that salt intake becomes more and more consistent, um, and improved.
[38:20] And so remember the typical amount we need in a day is about two
[38:26] teaspoons, give or take that's bare minimum.
[38:29] That is bare minimum for the body to function and do what it should do.
[38:35] Two teaspoons miraculously provides the right amount of sodium, the
[38:40] right amount of chloride and the right amount of iodine.
[38:44] If you're consuming iodized salt, not a huge fan of iodized salt.
[38:48] I don't, I don't want you getting the wrong impression, but patients
[38:52] should be aware they have a choice.
[38:54] They can use unhealthy iodized salt or they can use, oh, I didn't know
[38:59] that my husband's pillow was up there.
[39:01] Um, uh, or they can use an iodine supplement and the two
[39:05] teaspoons of healthy salt.
[39:07] Either way, uh, it's perfectly fine.
[39:10] But if they are going to get iodine from iodized salt, they absolutely
[39:15] have to get without fail, consistent two teaspoons every day in their
[39:20] food and drink, uh, and most of your electrolyte mixes do not include iodine.
[39:27] So keep that in mind.
[39:28] If they do, it's listed on the label as an ingredient and you won't find
[39:32] it in most of them because they're not using iodized salt for that.

[42:09] that got better outcomes on calcium supplementation than anything else.
[42:16] Like, yes, the bone density gets stronger, but it doesn't change fracture rate.
[42:23] It doesn't change morbidity, mortality rate.
[42:25] And so the outcomes data for bisphosphonates and calcium supplementation
[42:31] isn't that great, um, because both come with other problems.

[42:44] supplementation, let me share my screen so we can open the study up.

[42:59] And so, uh, this study goes back as far as 2011 in the British medical journal.
[43:05] Um, and so even, uh, 15 years ago, calcium supplements with or without vitamin D,
[43:14] um, showed, uh, poor benefit with the calcium.

[43:27] modestly increase the risk of cardiovascular events, especially
[43:31] myocardial infarction, modestly increase.

[43:37] Modiously, you sort of halfway have a heart attack, like the words
[43:42] that some of these studies use, uh, really, uh, baffle me.
[43:46] And so, um, uh, be very careful.

[43:54] them on a hundred to 200 milligrams twice a day, not 1200, which used to be
[44:01] the, the gold standard, um, uh, anything over about 600 milligrams of calcium
[44:08] supplementation is what's linked to atherosclerosis.

[50:36] Um, so the liver makes up about 2% of the total body weight.
[50:40] So if you have somebody that weighs a hundred pounds, about, um, 2% of
[50:46] that would be two pounds, right?
[50:49] Um, of total body weight is going to be liver.
[50:52] If you have somebody that weighs 300 pounds, that's probably going to be what?
[50:57] Six pounds, 2%, six pounds.
[51:00] So it's a fairly large organ.
[51:03] Um, if you think about it, uh, it does a bunch of stuff.
[51:07] It detoxes, it stores, it supplies cells with lots of substances.
[51:15] Uh, so it is massively important and we don't want to damage, um, the liver.
[51:21] So if you remember some of your basic A and P, there are zones to, um, the liver.
[51:31] And so, um, we are going to focus mostly on zone one's oxidative metabolism.
[51:40] Gluconeogenesis, bile, cholesterol, amino acid.
[51:43] That's, that's the majority of our metabolic function.
[51:47] Uh, we do talk a little bit about zone threes processes because we prescribe
[51:52] drugs, we want patients to detox.
[51:56] We want glycolysis to occur.
[51:58] We want lipogenesis to occur.
[52:00] And so some of our other, uh, uh, metabolic functions rely on zone three.
[52:06] So it's important that we kind of keep those in the back of our heads.
[52:10] Um, the space of this, um, is important because it houses a variety of, um,
[52:18] ingredients, collagen, proteoglycans, other proteins that help provide
[52:22] scaffolding for hepatocytes.
[52:24] So imagine if you will, um, how many of our paint I paint, like I've helped
[52:33] paint big projects before gyms and churches and lots of stuff.
[52:38] And so if you have, if I've ever painted or you've been on a construction
[52:42] site, scaffolding is a stack of metal, um, ladders and boards designed to get
[52:51] you 30, 40, 50, a hundred feet up in the air, like however high you need
[52:56] that scaffolding to go and it's, it's braced on the pieces below it.
[53:03] And so scaffolding is important for hepatocytes and the whole liver in general.
[53:09] So in the liver, your scaffolding is kind of like connective tissue is
[53:16] to your skin and your muscle.
[53:17] So like skin is held to your muscle by collagen.
[53:24] There's a whole collagenous matrix here.
[53:27] Otherwise I could just pull the top of my skin off and just come away.
[53:30] And there wouldn't be any muscle or tendon right under there.
[53:33] Okay.
[53:34] It just, it would be loose and floppy.
[53:37] Um, and so hepatocytes provide that, I mean, um, collagens and proteoglycans
[53:42] provide that kind of scaffolding and support the glue that holds all
[53:47] together for hepatocytes.
[53:48] And so, um, uh, it's important that liver work well for proteins to
[53:54] metabolize, they break down into amino acids.
[53:57] Um, uh, in that process, it produces a toxin called ammonia, and that goes
[54:02] through the kidneys to be paid off, uh, broken down and the water recycled
[54:08] and the urea, uh, paid off fat metabolism breaks down lipids.
[54:13] So we eat butter.
[54:15] We eat pork steak.
[54:17] That's fatty.
[54:18] We eat rib eyes.
[54:20] We eggs that have good, healthy cholesterol and fat.
[54:24] Um, and, um, all of that has to be broken down, uh, so that the phospholipid
[54:31] bilayer of cell membranes can take that up and use it as part of the outer membrane.
[54:37] Um, so it's fat metabolism is absolutely crucial for cholesterol
[54:42] health and cellular support and brain chemistry.
[54:49] Carbohydrate metabolism is fairly optional.
[54:53] Um, there's no agency, no organization, no expert, uh, guides anywhere that
[55:00] have ever said carbohydrates are essential to life.
[55:05] So remember, essential means that you must consume it for your body to have it,
[55:10] but carbohydrates are not essential because the body can make glucose
[55:14] from proteins or fats, um, or even our own muscle.
[55:18] Uh, it can take other molecules and break them further down into CHO.
[55:23] So, um, that's gluconeogenesis.
[55:26] The liver will produce whatever glucose is needed.
[55:29] Um, and so we used to call really wacky high levels of glucose for
[55:36] early in the morning fasting, the dawn phenomenon.
[55:39] We didn't know why in the morning glucose would randomly jump up.
[55:43] Now we know that it's the liver trying to help after overnight fasting.
[55:47] So you hit like three or four or five o'clock in the morning, you've been
[55:51] fasting all night cause you've been asleep.
[55:53] Um, but the body is used to having carbohydrates every two hours.
[55:57] And so it's now gone six or eight, 10 hours with no carbohydrate.
[56:02] And so the body says, Oh, well, I don't like my glucose falling to 75.
[56:07] Uh, I'm a, I'm going to spit out some glucose for you.
[56:09] So the liver releases that stored glycogen back into circulation as glucose.
[56:15] And that's what we see as dawn phenomenon.
[56:18] Um, Dom phenomenon is one of the hardest things to correct when we have patients
[56:26] with diabetes and, or insulin resistance.
[56:29] Dom phenomenon usually takes two to three years to completely clear up
[56:34] and stop bothering people.
[56:36] So never, never have your patients monitoring their fasting
[56:40] glucoses for this reason.
[56:43] Um, Dom phenomenon makes that glucose not be a very accurate number.
[56:48] The best way to test glucose and to monitor glucose is with, uh, test
[56:54] either a glucometer or a CGM before a meal and two hours after their meal
[56:59] and find the comparison there.
[57:02] Is there more than 10 or 12 points?
[57:04] If so, they had too many carbohydrates.
[57:06] If they're tracking protein conversion to glucose, you'll want to check
[57:11] before you eat two hours after for the glucose part, and then about
[57:16] four hours after for that protein gluconeogenesis process to hit the
[57:22] bloodstream, um, so you can check glucose at two hours and about four or
[57:26] five hours, um, to get both parts of the spike if you want to watch that.
[57:33] Um, so there's three major things that happen and the liver is the guide
[57:42] for most of it.
[57:44] Um, so if you, when we consume carbohydrates, they hit the mouth,
[57:51] they hit the bloodstream, they hit the gut, they hit the bloodstream and
[57:55] the bloodstream goes, Oh, too much sugar here.
[58:00] I can't deal with this.
[58:01] So the liver senses there's a problem with too much glucose in the bloodstream
[58:07] cause all that glucose really doesn't belong there.
[58:10] And so it shoots a message over to the pancreas and says, Hey, you got to
[58:15] help me get this stuff out of here.
[58:17] So the pancreas start spitting out insulin.
[58:19] How much you need Bubba, I'll send you a bunch.
[58:22] I'll send you a little, how much you need?
[58:24] So the pancreas is spitting out all this insulin.
[58:27] The insulin comes alongside and says, Hey, you're a glucose molecule.
[58:31] You can't stay here.
[58:32] You're poisoned to the bloodstream because of all this other glucose going on.
[58:37] You need to come out of here with me.
[58:38] You need to come over here and leave this bloodstream.
[58:43] And so that is what happens with weight management.
[58:51] Glucose gets stored as fat.
[58:54] Another process is also triggered about the same time.
[58:57] And the liver says, well, since there's so much around here, I'm just going to
[59:01] store some back here in the reserve gas tank, we're going to call this glycogen.
[59:06] And I'm just going to store some over here in the liver.
[59:08] That's what becomes fatty liver disease because that storage tank overflows.
[59:12] There's not enough room in the liver to store indefinitely.
[59:16] And so the suitcases, the tanks, they overflow, they overfill.
[59:22] And that's what is recognized on imaging as fatty liver disease.
[59:27] The other thing that the liver does for glucose management is it says, well,
[59:33] since there's so much around here, I'm putting a bunch of this into storage.
[59:37] I'm moving all this with insulin into fat cells, but I still have all
[59:42] this glucose letting around.
[59:44] I need to do something else.
[59:45] So I'm going to start knitting together triglyceride molecules.
[59:49] Tri is three, glyceride is glucose.
[59:52] So you have three glycerine molecules being knitted together to
[59:57] form a triglyceride fatty acid.
[59:59] And so that's another form of stored energy that can be released
[60:05] later, should the need arise.
[60:07] The problem is glucose, triglycerides, and insulin all wind up triggering
[60:14] cloddy, thick, sticky kinds of components in chemistry that end up
[60:22] resulting in atherosclerosis and clots.
[60:27] And so none of these things work out for the benefit when we are high
[60:32] glucose, high carb consumers.
[60:35] And so it's important that we understand all of the glucose management.
[60:40] I explained this really a different way to patients often, because
[60:45] this is more of a professional setting.
[60:49] I talk about it in relationship to my daddy eating peas.
[60:54] And so on my blog, on my joyfulhearthousecalls.com website, if you
[60:59] search the word peas or cornbread, you'll find my daddy's
[61:03] peas and cornbread article.
[61:04] And it really helps people understand what's happening with these
[61:09] three processes a little bit easier.
[61:11] So if you want to take a look at that, you certainly can.
[61:14] And you can share it with patients if you would like.
[61:18] So what is fatty liver?
[61:20] It's a big umbrella term, like non-alcoholic fatty liver disease,
[61:24] NASH, some cases of cirrhosis fall in this big umbrella term, but
[61:30] it's just early liver injury is all it is.
[61:32] And it winds up getting staged, I think it's five stages of liver
[61:38] damage when patients are found to have non-alcoholic fatty liver disease.
[61:45] So NASH is non-alcoholic Seattle hepatitis, big word NASH is much easier.
[61:50] It's a combination of fatty liver deposits and inflammation.
[61:55] So that's what makes it different from non-alcoholic fatty liver disease.
[62:01] Cirrhosis is the further, most advanced form of fatty liver disease, and it
[62:06] results in a lot of scar tissue and injured liver tissue that can't be fixed.
[62:13] Like once it's that far, it can't be fixed.
[62:17] Sometimes fatty liver will contribute to hepatic cancers and liver failure.
[62:25] So just be aware that there is a huge progression.
[62:30] It is easy to reverse as long as you're not into cirrhotic stage yet.
[62:40] So how do you diagnose fatty liver disease?
[62:42] Well, mostly by imaging, but why would you want to order imaging?
[62:48] These are the reasons.
[62:49] Look at their diabetes state, their insulin resistance, PCOS, PMOS, what's
[62:55] their A1C, what is their insulin level?
[62:59] What is their waist circumference?
[63:01] Is there that central obesity?
[63:03] Do they have imaging, maybe an MRI or a CT scan of their abdomen that shows visceral
[63:10] fat? Is their blood pressure pretty high?
[63:14] What's their triglycerides?
[63:16] What's their metabolic state in general?
[63:18] So all of these things should trigger, oh, I wonder if there's fatty liver.
[63:22] So these are the things you keep in the back of your mind when you see liver
[63:25] enzymes that are like ASC and ALT that are 30, 40, 50.
[63:31] I wonder if this patient is developing fatty liver disease.
[63:35] They have an ALT-FOS running in the hundreds or higher.
[63:40] Ideal ALT-FOS, remember, is about 60.
[63:43] Ideal AST ALT is about 20 each.
[63:46] So if you have numbers higher than that, even though they might be in normal
[63:50] range, you may have found early liver disease and you may want to help your
[63:57] patient understand what those numbers mean and how they can reduce that
[64:01] stress and improve liver health.
[64:06] You can use those CMP markers, but they are indicative, not diagnostic.
[64:11] Your only gold standard is imaging.
[64:18] OK, so you want to get an image that the levels, blood levels might
[64:23] suggest fatty liver, but the gold standard is imaging.
[64:28] And you can use any of the imaging if you are sending them for x-rays
[64:34] of their back or they're getting MRIs of their hip, they're going to have
[64:40] some evidence of their belly on there.
[64:44] If you're getting CTs of, you know, whatever, they're of their of their
[64:49] belly, pay attention to other things it says.
[64:53] Many, many prescribers will send patients for whatever imaging they're
[64:59] doing, but they'll they'll only pay attention to the notable findings.
[65:03] They won't look at the narrative.
[65:05] They won't really see anything that might suggest there's fatty
[65:11] liver or liver injury present.
[65:13] So be very aware that all of that writing is important.
[65:18] And so read through it carefully and use your clinical judgment.
[65:24] But the gold standard is to diagnose fatty liver when you see it,
[65:28] not when you suspect it.
[65:30] I've had a boatload of patients come to me with a diagnosis of fatty
[65:34] liver. And when I asked them how they were diagnosed, my blood test
[65:38] like, no, we can't diagnose it with a blood test.
[65:41] Fatty liver is something you see, not something you draw blood for.
[65:47] Fatty liver must be determined by pictures.
[65:50] And this is why you can't see the difference between a healthy liver
[65:56] and a fatty liver on a belly.
[65:58] You can sometimes palpate if you are palpating and seeing patients
[66:04] in person, you can palpate their belly.
[66:06] You can when you're when you're feeling on their liver,
[66:10] you can feel the margins of the liver sometimes on your smaller patients.
[66:15] If they're morbidly obese and they have a habitus that's large,
[66:19] you may not be able to assess the liver via palpation,
[66:23] but you can sometimes find the margins on a liver
[66:27] and suspect that it's enlarged.
[66:29] And that would be another indication of some fatty liver disease.
[66:33] So you can use some anti diabetic drugs.
[66:37] We see, like I mentioned earlier in our session tonight,
[66:41] GLP ones are becoming massively beneficial for early fatty liver disease
[66:47] and other inflammatory conditions.
[66:51] And so definitely think about all of these possibilities
[66:56] when you are managing fatty liver disease.
[66:59] In some of the studies, if we lose at least five percent.
[67:03] So if you have a patient that weighs 250 pounds and they lose five percent,
[67:08] that's what 10 percent would be 25 pounds.
[67:13] So that's only 12 pounds of weight.
[67:15] That showed improvement in their fatty liver disease.
[67:18] Weight loss of just five percent.
[67:21] So what do you think is going to happen if they lose 15 or 20 percent
[67:24] of their body weight?
[67:25] If they're weighing 250, 300 pounds or more
[67:29] and they lose five percent of their body weight,
[67:32] they're losing a large amount of weight.
[67:36] But if they continue that effort and they lose more,
[67:40] then the pathologic changes improve long term.
[67:46] And I recently saw a study.
[67:49] I was reading something that showed.
[67:51] I don't know if I kept it in this PowerPoint or not,
[67:54] but it mentioned with some GOP ones.
[67:58] It was one of the GOP one reports.
[68:01] It talked about even though.
[68:05] The patient used to GOP one to lose weight.
[68:08] They got healthier and better and they got off their medicine,
[68:12] even if they gained weight back, which happens with every diet.
[68:16] Keep that in mind.
[68:19] When they did not gain visceral fat back,
[68:23] their liver benefits remained the same.
[68:28] So that was remarkable.
[68:30] Visceral fat is what is so unhealthy for our bodies.
[68:34] We can have skeletal fat stored.
[68:37] We can have, you know, a large amount of weight stored on our body.
[68:43] But the visceral fat that's the fat surrounding
[68:48] organs within the body, that fat is what's more dangerous
[68:54] because it's stressing those organs as it continues to grow
[68:58] and it begins to sometimes even compress on that tissue.
[69:02] So the American Association of Family Provider,

[69:10] It's American Academy of Family Practitioner, family.

[69:31] does recommend GOP ones as one of your treatment plans for fatty liver disease.
[69:37] So exercise is beneficial, but not for weight loss.
[69:43] So exercise is how our body communicates
[69:47] with different parts of the system.

[69:56] So patients don't have to go to the gym.

[70:33] helps communicate with the pituitary gland, the thyroid
[70:37] and insulin management pancreas release of insulin
[70:41] to help with endocrine function.
[70:45] So based on functions of the liver,

[70:52] If we reduce the carbs, the insulin is no longer going to store adipose, right?
[70:58] It's going to not keep storing weight.
[71:01] If we reduce carbs, glycogen stores are reduced.
[71:06] If we reduce carbs, triglycerides are also significantly lowered.
[71:11] And so like the the common sense
[71:15] management for fatty liver disease is low carb.
[71:20] If you cut out the carbs,
[71:22] there's no reason for any of the liver stress to occur.
[71:27] So that's the easiest way to get patients
[71:31] to get their livers healthier is just cut the carbs.
